notes.anchor: C. CASSIVS PROCOS. S. D. M. CICERONI
notes.note: C. Cassius Longinus, the tyrannicide, writes as PROCOS. (proconsul) in command of the republican forces in the East. The formula S. D. is salutem dicit, "sends greetings," to M. Cicero.

notes.anchor: If you are well, that is good; I myself am well.
notes.note: Renders the stock epistolary opening abbreviated in the Latin as S. v. b. e. e. q. v. (si vales, bene est; ego quoque valeo), a formula of well-wishing that conventionally began a Roman letter.

notes.anchor: L. Murcus and Q. Crispus, commanders
notes.note: L. Statius Murcus and Q. Marcius Crispus, the two governors with armies in Syria who joined Cassius; "commanders" renders imp. (imperator), the victory-title their troops had given them.

notes.anchor: the legion which Q. Caecilius Bassus had
notes.note: Q. Caecilius Bassus was a renegade Pompeian who had seized a legion in Syria and held out against Caesarian forces; in 43 BC it went over to Cassius.

notes.anchor: the four legions which A. Allienus led out of Egypt
notes.note: A. Allienus had marched four legions from Egypt intended for Dolabella; he handed them to Cassius instead, a major accession of strength for the republican cause.

notes.anchor: Issued on the Nones of March, from the camp at Tarichea.
notes.note: The Nones of March = 7 March (43 BC). "Tarichea" (the manuscripts vary) marks a camp in or near Syria; the place is not securely identified.
